title: Cyphostemma humile
taxonomy: Cyphostemma humile is classified in the plant kingdom as a member of the Apocynaceae family, which is a large family of flowering plants commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. This classification is based on its morphology and phylogenetic relationships. Cyphostemma humile is further classified as a genus within the Asclepiadoideae subfamily. It is native to tropical and subtropical regions, and its classification reflects its adaptation to these environments. The exact classification may vary depending on the source and the latest taxonomic research.

side_effects: Cyphostemma humile, also known as the small grape or baby raisin vine, is used in traditional medicine and has been reported to have various side effects. The plant contains toxic compounds that can cause vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain when ingested in large quantities. It can also interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners, and may exacerbate conditions like liver disease. Furthermore, the plant's toxic compounds can cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. Prolonged use or excessive consumption of Cyphostemma humile can lead to more severe side effects, including liver damage and kidney problems.
intro: Cyphostemma humile, also known as the "baby's choker," is a perennial succulent plant native to southern Africa, belonging to the grapevine family (Vitaceae). The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its various health benefits, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of hypertension, diabetes, and gastrointestinal disorders. The bioactive constituents of Cyphostemma humile include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and triterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Cyphostemma humile is often prepared as a decoction or infused in a tea, and sometimes used to make a tincture or tonic, which are used to treat a range of health conditions.
